english: =snyéṅ-ba (snyeng ba) to stretch one's self, to stretch forward the neck; bya-rmyáṅ byéd-pa (bya rmyang byed pa) id. Cs. also: to yawn.
beard Mil., smá-ra-čan (sma ra can) bearded.
1. a sort of medicine of an astringent taste Med.; smág-rgyu (smag rgyu) black pepper. — 2. dark; darkness; mún-pai smag-rúm (mun pai smag rum) id. Glr.

अधर, 1. the lower part, opp. to stod (stod); smád-la (smad la) downward Sch.; lús-kyi smad (lus kyi smad) the lower half of the human body, frq.; smad °pyés-pa (smad 'phyes pa) Sch.: 'to move the posterior to and fro' (?). — lus-smad-lṅa sá-la γtúg-pa (lus smad lnga sa la gtug pa) to bring the five lower parts of the body, the belly, the knees, and the points of the feet in close contact with the ground, i.e. to prostrate one's self; hence čos-gos smad lṅa (chos gos smad lnga) Dzl. 720, 16, the five lower pieces of the priestly apparel, perh. breeches, stockings and boots; the meaning, however, of sems-smád bčo-brgyád (sems smad bco brgyad) Pth. I am not prepared to settle — 2. lowland = man-čád (man chad). — 3. low rank, v. smad-rigs (smad rigs) below. — 4. with regard to time, the latter part, the second half, उत्तर, of the night, Dzl., of winter, of life etc. — 5. children, in relation to their mother, gen. preceded by ma (ma) or bu (bu), thus: ṅed ma-smád (nged ma smad) I and my mother Mil.; rgán-mo ma-smád γsum (rgan mo ma smad gsum) the old woman with her (two) sons, those three Dzl.; also of animals: rgód-ma ma-smád γnyis (rgod ma ma smad gnyis) the mare and her foal, the two Dzl.; bu-smád (bu smad) (Cs. also mad (mad)) wife and children, family; nád-pa deí bu-smád (nad pa dei bu smad) Mil. the sick man's family; bu-smád-rnams (bu smad rnams) (my) wife and children Mil. Comp. smad-°čal (smad 'chal) lewdness, dissoluteness, prostitution, byéd-pa (byed pa) to indulge in, to practise Mil. — smad-°dógs (smad 'dogs) a subscribed letter Gram. — smád-tsoṅ-ma (smad tshong ma) 'meretrix', prostitute, harlot, frq. — smad-γyógs (smad g-yogs) nether integuments, breeches, trowsers Wdṅ. — smad-rígs (smad rigs) common people, lower caste Dzl.

I. vb. 1. to bend down; to hand, to reach down, the alms bowl to a little boy Dzl.; (Sch. 'to stoop'?); γdoṅ smád-pa (gdong smad pa) to cast down one's eyes, to be abashed, dejected Tar.; sems (sems) to humble one's self, la (la) before Dzl., tugs (thugs) id. resp.: to be condescending, lowly, meek Dzl. — 2. to vilify, c. la (la) or accus.: to blame, to chide, bú-mo (bu mo) one's own daughter Dzl., bdág-gi séms-la (bdag gi sems la) to blame one's self Dzl; to abuse, defame, degrade, traduce, tsig ṅár-pas °págs-pa-la (tshig ngar pas 'phags pa la) (to abuse) the venerable man with base words Dzl., dkon-mčóg-gi dbu-°páṅ (dkon mchog gi dbu 'phang) (to degrade) the highness of the excellent, = to blaspheme; to despise, the doctrine Glr.; to dishonour, violate, ravish, bu-moí lus (bu moi lus) a girl Pth.; má-ga-dha nyáms-smad-pai tse (ma ga dha nyams smad pai tshe) Tar. 192 when (the country of) Magadha had been brought low, had decayed in its prosperity; smád-pai tsig (smad pai tshig) or ṅag (ngag) abusive word, invective, libel; smád-ra (smad ra) (prop. sgra (sgra)) id., more in the language of the common people, but also Mil.; smád-ra γtóṅ-ba (smad ra gtong ba) Mil., *taṅ-če* (*tang che*) W. to abuse, to revile; smad-rigs (smad rigs) common people. II. sbst. blame, reproof, reproach, disgrace, contempt.

1. medicine, physic, remedy, both artificially prepared and crude: medicinal herb, drug; rií sman °tú-ba (rii sman 'thu ba) to gather officinal plants on the mountains Dzl.; mén-la °do* (men la 'do*) C., *man-la ča* (*man la cha*) W, (the plant) is used as a medicine; sman sbyór-ba (sman sbyor ba) to prepare a medicine, γtóṅ-ba (gtong ba) to administer, zá-ba (za ba) or °túṅ-ba ('thung ba) to take (physic); different forms of medicine are: táṅ-gi sman (thang gi sman) liquid medicine, infusion, decocture; °pyé-mai sman ('phye mai sman) powder; ríl-bu (ril bu) pill; ldé-gu (lde gu) electuary, sirup; sman-már (sman mar) oily medicine (Tar. 39, 8); sman-čáṅ (sman chang) prob. alcoholic tincture; °bri-ta ('bri ta) extract(?). — Further: koṅ-sman (khong sman) medicine taken internally, byúg-sman (byug sman) used externally, unguent; °byár-sman ('byar sman) plaster; bzí-sman (bzi sman) soporiferous potion; skyúg-sman (skyug sman) emetic; bšál-sman (bshal sman) purgative. — smán-gyi bla (sman gyi bla), or smán-bla (sman bla) Glr. and Med.; Sch.: 'physician general', yet to my knowledge it is never used in that sense, but only as a god or Buddha of therapeutics; there are eight such gods,
